claim: Willison argues YOLO mode plus a remote sandbox is the right shape: 'the best sandboxes run on someone else's computer', and network restrictions cut off the data-exfiltration leg of the lethal trifecta.
quote: The best sandboxes run on someone else's computer... cuts off the data exfiltration leg of the lethal trifecta.

claim: The 'lethal trifecta' — private data access + untrusted content + external communication — is Willison's framing for why network isolation is the most important mitigation for YOLO agents.
quote: Anyone who gets text into your LLM has full control over what tools it runs next.

claim: Anthropic's official position is that filesystem + network isolation together are required, and that sandboxing reduces permission prompts by 84% in internal usage while fully containing successful prompt injections.
quote: Effective sandboxing requires both filesystem and network isolation... sandboxing ensures that even a successful prompt injection is fully isolated, and cannot impact overall user security.

claim: Anthropic's March 2026 auto-mode post admits users approve 93% of permission prompts — the empirical basis for the 'permission fatigue makes prompts theatre' argument.
quote: Approval fatigue, where people stop paying close attention to what they're approving.

claim: Anthropic's security docs explicitly tell users to run Claude Code in VMs or devcontainers for untrusted content, and warn that no system is fully immune to prompt injection.
quote: Use virtual machines (VMs) to run scripts and make tool calls, especially when interacting with external web services... While these protections significantly reduce risk, no system is completely immune to all attacks.

claim: Anthropic's own docs contradict each other: Best Practices says YOLO requires 'a sandbox without internet access', but the Devcontainer docs say firewall rules are sufficient — the issue was closed 'not planned'.
quote: Only use --dangerously-skip-permissions in a sandbox without internet access... The container's enhanced security measures (isolation and firewall rules) allow you to run claude --dangerously-skip-permissions.

claim: UpGuard's audit of 18,470 public Claude config files found 22.2% granted Bash(rm:*), 21.3% granted curl, and only 1.1% used deny/ask rules — evidence that the 'permissions' layer is mostly performative.
quote: A shocking 22.2% of files (4,101 users) granted the Bash(rm:*) permission... Only 1.1% of files used [deny and ask rules].

claim: Multiple documented 2025 incidents of Claude Code running destructive rm -rf that hit users' home directories via shell tilde expansion — the canonical 'sandbox would have saved me' case.
quote: rm -rf tests/ patches/ plan/ ~/ - See that ~/ at the end? That's your entire home directory.

claim: Invariant Labs' May 2025 GitHub MCP prompt-injection proved an agent with broad PAT access can be hijacked by a public issue into leaking private repo contents via a public PR.
quote: When a developer innocently asks their AI assistant to 'check the open issues,' the agent reads the malicious issue, gets prompt-injected, and follows hidden instructions to access private repositories and leak sensitive data publicly.

claim: Cyata found three CVEs (CVE-2025-68143/68144/68145) in Anthropic's own Git MCP server, chainable to RCE via git smudge/clean filters — the 'toxic combination' problem with MCP composability.
quote: Each MCP server might look safe in isolation, but combine two of them... and you get a toxic combination.

claim: April 2026 Register coverage: researchers say MCP's STDIO handling is an architectural flaw enabling unauthenticated command injection and zero-click prompt injection across 200k+ servers; Anthropic declined to change the protocol, calling the behavior 'expected'.
quote: it actually lets anyone run any arbitrary OS command, if the command successfully creates an STDIO server it will return the handle.

claim: Check Point's CVE-2025-59536 and CVE-2026-21852 showed that a malicious .claude/settings.json in a cloned repo could execute RCE via hooks and exfiltrate the API key via ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL before the user even saw the trust dialog.
quote: our command executed immediately upon running claude – before the user could even read the trust dialog... their API key would be sent directly to the attacker's server.

claim: Trail of Bits' October 2025 research demonstrated prompt-injection-to-RCE against three agent platforms including Claude Code, bypassing human approval gates — evidence that per-action prompts alone are not a security boundary.
quote: bypassed human approval protections for system command execution in AI agents, achieving RCE in three agent platforms.

claim: March 2026 community research synthesises the 2026 consensus: the sandbox is the only layer that catches novel attacks because it prevents the outcome rather than recognising the command, while deny rules are 'a friction layer, not a security boundary'.
quote: The sandbox is the only layer that catches novel attacks — because it doesn't need to recognize the command, it prevents the outcome... Deny rules are a friction layer, not a security boundary.
